ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper applies a semi-supervised graph-based (max-flow/min-cut) classification paradigm to time-tag WordNet synsets, which—according to the authors—is the first work to use a graph-based algorithm exploiting WordNet glosses, relations, and attributes for this task. The problem (temporal orientation of word senses) has prior work (Dias et al. 2014; Hasanuzzaman et al. 2014b) but those approaches relied mostly on glosses and non-graph classifiers (e.g., SVMs), so the graph formulation and exploitation of WordNet relations represent a clear methodological departure. Constructing a crowdsourced gold standard and demonstrating empirical gains over prior semi-supervised, non-graph methods further differentiates the contribution. Overall this is a notable and non-trivial novelty rather than a purely incremental combination of existing techniques. | B(4): The paper introduces a notable new methodological angle by applying a semi‑supervised graph‑based (max‑flow/min‑cut) classification paradigm to time‑tag WordNet synsets, which the authors claim is the first work to exploit WordNet glosses, relations, and attributes together for temporal orientation. This clearly differentiates it from prior gloss‑focused, SVM‑based approaches (e.g., Dias et al. 2014) and represents a non‑trivial extension in technique. The creation of a new crowdsourced gold standard (398 synsets) and reported outperformance of prior non‑graph methods further supports that the contribution would be informative to experts, though the overall problem (temporal orientation) has been previously studied.

The problem (temporal orientation of word senses) has prior work (Dias et al. 2014; Hasanuzzaman et al. 2014b) but those approaches relied mostly on glosses and non-graph classifiers (e.g., SVMs), so the graph formulation and exploitation of WordNet relations represent a clear methodological departure. Constructing a crowdsourced gold standard and demonstrating empirical gains over prior semi-supervised, non-graph methods further differentiates the contribution. Overall this is a notable and non-trivial novelty rather than a purely incremental combination of existing techniques.

The creation of a new crowdsourced gold standard (398 synsets) and reported outperformance of prior non‑graph methods further supports that the contribution would be informative to experts, though the overall problem (temporal orientation) has been previously studied.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): Important reproducibility details are missing: hyperparameters and model training/configuration settings are not reported, and code availability is not mentioned. While core datasets like WordNet and the TempoWordNet set are identifiable, the authors’ CrowdFlower gold standard (398 synsets) and preprocessing pipeline are not clearly described or stated as public. The method is described at a high level (graph-based min-cut) and evaluation metrics (percent agreement, annotator accuracy) are reported, but many implementation and data details required to reproduce the results are absent. | B(3): Important elements for replication are missing: hyperparameters and model/training configurations are not reported and code is not mentioned. The data sources (WordNet, TempoWordNet) are public or cited, but the authors' CrowdFlower gold standard availability is unclear and preprocessing is not described. The algorithmic approach (graph-based min-cut) and evaluation metrics (percentage agreement, intrinsic/extrinsic evaluations) are specified, enabling partial reimplementation, but several implementation and data-details needed for full reproducibility are absent.

The method is described at a high level (graph-based min-cut) and evaluation metrics (percent agreement, annotator accuracy) are reported, but many implementation and data details required to reproduce the results are absent.

The data sources (WordNet, TempoWordNet) are public or cited, but the authors' CrowdFlower gold standard availability is unclear and preprocessing is not described. The algorithmic approach (graph-based min-cut) and evaluation metrics (percentage agreement, intrinsic/extrinsic evaluations) are specified, enabling partial reimplementation, but several implementation and data-details needed for full reproducibility are absent.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments comparing the proposed max-flow/min-cut graph method against relevant baselines (TempoWordNet variants and UTTime) using both intrinsic (crowdsourced 398-synset gold standard and Dias et al. data) and extrinsic (TempEval-3) evaluations. There are no formal mathematical proofs presented in the summary but the algorithmic approach (graph min-cuts) is standard and plausible. Experimental methodology is broadly sound in using prior labeled data and appropriate baselines, but important details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance tests, and a relatively small crowdsourced test set), which weakens confidence in the robustness of the performance claims. Overall the conclusions are reasonable given the reported results, though the omitted experimental details are a minor but notable limitation. | B(4): The core claim—using a graph-based min-cut semi-supervised method to time-tag WordNet synsets—is supported by experiments on TempoWordNet, a newly crowdsourced 398-synset gold standard, control synsets, and extrinsic comparison on TempEval-3, and evaluated against relevant baselines (Dias et al., Hasanuzzaman et al., UTTime). However, the summary does not report mathematical derivations or proofs in detail, and experimental reporting omits hyperparameter settings, code availability, and statistical significance testing, which are missing pieces though not fatal to the main conclusions. The authors acknowledge intrinsic limitations (ambiguity of temporal orientation) and use reasonable quality controls (control synsets and rater threshold), so the methodology is largely sound with minor reporting gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments comparing the proposed max-flow/min-cut graph method against relevant baselines (TempoWordNet variants and UTTime) using both intrinsic (crowdsourced 398-synset gold standard and Dias et al. data) and extrinsic (TempEval-3) evaluations. There are no formal mathematical proofs presented in the summary but the algorithmic approach (graph min-cuts) is standard and plausible. Experimental methodology is broadly sound in using prior labeled data and appropriate baselines, but important details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance tests, and a relatively small crowdsourced test set), which weakens confidence in the robustness of the performance claims. Overall the conclusions are reasonable given the reported results, though the omitted experimental details are a minor but notable limitation.

reviewer_b.justification: The core claim—using a graph-based min-cut semi-supervised method to time-tag WordNet synsets—is supported by experiments on TempoWordNet, a newly crowdsourced 398-synset gold standard, control synsets, and extrinsic comparison on TempEval-3, and evaluated against relevant baselines (Dias et al., Hasanuzzaman et al., UTTime). However, the summary does not report mathematical derivations or proofs in detail, and experimental reporting omits hyperparameter settings, code availability, and statistical significance testing, which are missing pieces though not fatal to the main conclusions. The authors acknowledge intrinsic limitations (ambiguity of temporal orientation) and use reasonable quality controls (control synsets and rater threshold), so the methodology is largely sound with minor reporting gaps.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses a useful problem—time-tagging WordNet synsets—and, to the authors' knowledge, is the first to apply a semi-supervised graph-based (max-flow min-cut) method that leverages WordNet glosses, relations and attributes. It reports better intrinsic and extrinsic performance than prior non-graph semi-supervised approaches, and contributes a crowdsourced gold standard (398 synsets) while evaluating against TempoWordNet and TempEval-3, indicating practical utility. However, the intrinsic ambiguity of synset temporality and the authors' own note that past/present/future labels are often indicative rather than strict limits its broader, transformative impact, so the contribution is moderate rather than field-changing. | B(3): The paper tackles a relevant subproblem (temporal orientation of WordNet synsets) and appears to be the first to apply a graph-based max-flow/min-cut semi-supervised method to it, showing improvements over prior non-graph semi-supervised approaches and demonstrating extrinsic utility via TempEval-3 comparisons. The authors also contribute a new crowdsourced gold standard (398 synsets) and leverage existing TempoWordNet resources, which can seed follow-up work. However, the contribution is somewhat niche (focused on WordNet sense tagging), constrained by inherent sense ambiguity and difficulty distinguishing past/present/future, and relies on relatively small annotated sets, so its impact is moderate rather than transformative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): Yes — the work is squarely within NLP temporality/lexical semantics and uses methods (semi-supervised graph min-cut over WordNet glosses/relations) and evaluations (intrinsic + extrinsic; TempEval-3 comparisons) that the top NLP/ML community expects. It compares to appropriate baselines (TempoWordNet, SVM-based approaches, UTTime), constructs a crowdsourced gold standard, and uses standard resources (WordNet, TempoWordNet), so the audience and positioning are appropriate for a top-tier venue. | B(5): This is squarely within the scope of top NLP venues: it addresses temporality of word senses, uses standard lexical resources (WordNet), and evaluates both intrinsically and extrinsically (including TempEval-3) against prior baselines (TempoWordNet, TWnP/TWnH, UTTime). The semi-supervised graph-based (min-cut) approach and the constructed crowdsourced gold standard are appropriate methods and artifacts for the NLP research community. The problem formulation, datasets, and comparisons target the right audience and are positioned correctly within NLP temporality and lexical semantics research.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized (clear progression from problem statement to a graph-based method and intrinsic/extrinsic experiments) and states its main contributions early (semi-supervised max-flow/min-cut approach to temporally tag WordNet synsets). Writing quality is described as adequate and related work coverage is sufficient, and results/tables are reported with concrete metrics (agreement percentages) which suggests effective presentation of experiments. Minor presentation issues remain: the summary does not indicate whether formal notation is defined consistently or whether hyperparameters and some methodological details are fully reported, so a small amount of clarification or additional detail would improve clarity. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from introduction to method (a max-flow min-cut graph-based classifier) to experiments (intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations) and conclusion, and its main contributions (a graph-based temporality classifier and a crowdsourced gold standard) are stated early. Notation and methodological description seem consistent enough to follow the algorithmic approach, and related work, datasets (WordNet, TempoWordNet, TempEval-3), and evaluation metrics are described adequately. Presentation weaknesses are minor: effectiveness of figures/tables is not reported in the summary and hyperparameter/code reporting is missing, and some wording about limitations (e.g., borderline temporal cases) leaves residual ambiguity despite generally adequate writing.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (TempoWordNet/Dias et al. 2014, TWnP/TWnH Hasanuzzaman et al. 2014b, prior SVM-based approaches, and UTTime for extrinsic comparisons) and uses the same TempoWordNet data and TempEval-3 for external task evaluation. Differences between the graph-based min-cut method and prior gloss/SVM approaches are discussed (use of WordNet relations, critique of SVM probability calibration). However, hyperparameter reporting is missing and the summary does not indicate the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so comparisons are good but with minor omissions on fairness/transparency. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (TempoWordNet from Dias et al. 2014, TWnP/TWnH from Hasanuzzaman et al. 2014b, SVM-based prior semi-supervised methods, and UTTime for extrinsic TempEval-3 comparisons) and evaluates on shared resources (TempoWordNet, a crowdsourced gold standard, and TempEval-3 following guidelines). Comparisons appear fair (intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations, same datasets/metrics like percentage agreement) and the authors explicitly discuss how their graph-based max-flow/min-cut approach differs from and addresses limitations of prior gloss/SVM-based methods. The paper claims outperforming prior semi-supervised non-graph approaches and cites/discusses the main competing works, though it is not clearly stated whether they acknowledge any cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, a minor omission.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (TempoWordNet/Dias et al. 2014, TWnP/TWnH Hasanuzzaman et al. 2014b, prior SVM-based approaches, and UTTime for extrinsic comparisons) and uses the same TempoWordNet data and TempEval-3 for external task evaluation. Differences between the graph-based min-cut method and prior gloss/SVM approaches are discussed (use of WordNet relations, critique of SVM probability calibration). However, hyperparameter reporting is missing and the summary does not indicate the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so comparisons are good but with minor omissions on fairness/transparency.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (TempoWordNet from Dias et al. 2014, TWnP/TWnH from Hasanuzzaman et al. 2014b, SVM-based prior semi-supervised methods, and UTTime for extrinsic TempEval-3 comparisons) and evaluates on shared resources (TempoWordNet, a crowdsourced gold standard, and TempEval-3 following guidelines). Comparisons appear fair (intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations, same datasets/metrics like percentage agreement) and the authors explicitly discuss how their graph-based max-flow/min-cut approach differs from and addresses limitations of prior gloss/SVM-based methods. The paper claims outperforming prior semi-supervised non-graph approaches and cites/discusses the main competing works, though it is not clearly stated whether they acknowledge any cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, a minor omission.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper runs both intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations, uses multiple datasets (TempoWordNet, a new 398-synset crowdsourced gold standard, control synsets, and TempEval-3 comparisons) and compares against several baselines (TWn, TWnP/TWnH, UTTime), which supports the main performance claims. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are reported and hyperparameters/code are not provided, limiting assessment of robustness and reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as adequate and paper discusses failure cases and limitations (e.g., sense ambiguity and difficulty of past/present/future labeling). Given these strengths and the notable gaps in analysis and reproducibility, the evidence is adequate but incomplete. | B(3): The paper presents solid empirical evaluation (intrinsic and extrinsic tests, comparisons to TempoWordNet and other baselines, use of a crowdsourced 398-synset gold standard plus the 632/632 Dias et al. set, and TempEval-3 for external comparisons), and it explicitly discusses limitations of the task. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are reported and hyperparameter details and code availability are not provided, which weakens reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but the missing experimental analyses and reproducibility details justify a middle rating.

However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are reported and hyperparameters/code are not provided, limiting assessment of robustness and reproducibility. Given these strengths and the notable gaps in analysis and reproducibility, the evidence is adequate but incomplete.

However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are reported and hyperparameter details and code availability are not provided, which weakens reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but the missing experimental analyses and reproducibility details justify a middle rating.

main_claims: Proposes a semi-supervised graph-based classification paradigm (min-cuts / max-flow min-cut) to automatically time-tag WordNet synsets as past, present, future, or atemporal.

Presents, to the authors' knowledge, the first work aiming to recognize temporal dimension of word senses via a graph-based classification algorithm that exploits WordNet glosses, relations, and attributes.
Reports that intrinsic and extrinsic evaluation results show the proposed approach outperforms prior semi-supervised, non-graph classification approaches to temporality recognition of word senses.
Constructs a crowdsourced gold standard of annotated synsets (398 synsets) and uses an existing labeled set of 632 temporal and 632 atemporal synsets from Dias et al. (2014) for experiments.
_summary.methodology: A semi-supervised graph-based classification method built on the max-flow min-cut optimization (mincuts) that uses WordNet definitions (glosses), conceptual-semantic and lexical relations, and other WordNet attributes to label synsets as past/present/future/atemporal.
datasets: WordNet (as source of synsets, glosses, relations)
TempoWordNet labeled set from Dias et al. (2014): 632 temporal synsets and 632 atemporal synsets
CrowdFlower-annotated gold standard: 398 synsets (created by the authors)
Control synsets from Dias et al. (2014): 60 control synsets
TempEval-3 datasets (used following guidelines to produce comparative results with UTTime, as stated)
baselines: Prior semi-supervised non-graph classification approaches (including SVM-based methods referenced)
UTTime (Laokulrat et al., 2013) (for comparative external task results)
metrics: Percentage agreement (observed agreement; e.g., 84.83% for temporal vs atemporal, 72.36% for past/present/future)
Annotator accuracy on control synsets (threshold of 70% accuracy used for rater reliability)
Intrinsic and extrinsic evaluation (types of evaluation reported)
limitations: Temporal orientation of synsets is often ambiguous and judged mainly from glosses; some synsets (e.g., 'dinosaur') can be classified as temporal or atemporal based on their definition.

Classifying temporal synsets into past, present, or future is more difficult than temporal vs atemporal because these connotations are indicative rather than strict classes; many temporal synsets are neither strictly past, present, nor future (e.g., 'monthly').

Prior approaches' limitations noted: reliance mostly on glosses without effectively exploiting WordNet relations, and use of SVM-derived probabilities without post-calibration (stated as disadvantages of earlier work).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - temporality / temporal orientation of WordNet word senses
